items.prompt_md: In the greedy reachability scan, what is the **only** piece of state the algorithm needs to carry forward between positions?
options: The farthest index reached so far
A boolean array marking every reachable index
The minimum number of jumps taken so far
The current index's own jump value
items.answer: The farthest index reached so far
items.explanation_md: Every reachable index is dominated by the farthest one seen so far — an earlier reachable index can always under-jump to match it, so it adds no new information. One running maximum is enough.

items.prompt_md: You're given a per-position 'max step' capacity and must decide, yes or no, whether you can travel from the first index to the last. Which approach fits in a single O(n) pass?
options: A DP table marking every index's reachability individually
Binary search for the largest single jump in the array
Track a running farthest-reachable index and fail as soon as the current index passes it
Backtrack over every possible sequence of jumps
items.answer: Track a running farthest-reachable index and fail as soon as the current index passes it
items.explanation_md: Reachable indices always form a prefix dominated by the farthest one seen so far, so a single running maximum — extended greedily at each step — replaces the whole DP table.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the one-pass farthest-reach solution to Jump Game?
options: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
items.explanation_md: One forward scan of the array, updating a single running maximum at each index — no auxiliary table, so it's O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
